question.text: Government securities are considered to have no credit risk because:

answer.text: They offer the highest coupon rate in the market
They are always issued at a premium to face value
They are backed by the collateral of government assets
They are backed by the sovereign guarantee of the government, making default virtually impossible

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Government securities (G-Secs) carry no credit/default risk because they are backed by the sovereign guarantee of the Government of India. The government can always meet its debt obligations through taxation or money creation, making default virtually impossible. This is why G-Secs are the benchmark for risk-free rates. **The Trap:** Option A is wrong — G-Secs typically offer lower coupon rates than corporate bonds precisely because they carry no credit risk. Option C is wrong because G-Secs are not backed by specific collateral; the sovereign guarantee is different from asset-backed security. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series IV (Interest Rate Derivatives) and Series V-B (MF Foundation) extensively use G-Sec yields as the risk-free benchmark. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Sovereign guarantee means zero default risk, but G-Secs still carry interest rate risk and inflation risk. No investment is completely "risk-free" — clarify this to clients.
